content.text: Applying agile-at-scale frameworks to GenAI programs requires deliberate adaptation. SAFe, LeSS, and Scrum@Scale were designed for software delivery, and while their fundamental principles — iterative delivery, cross-functional teams, continuous improvement — apply to GenAI work, the nature of AI development introduces complications that these frameworks did not originally anticipate. Model nondeterminism, long training cycles, data dependency chains, and evaluation-driven quality gates require specific structural adaptations to make these frameworks effective for AI programs.
What It Is

SAFe (Scaled Agile Framework), LeSS (Large-Scale Scrum), and Scrum@Scale are frameworks for coordinating multiple agile teams working toward shared goals. Each has a distinct philosophy: SAFe is prescriptive and process-heavy, providing a comprehensive structure from portfolio level to team level; LeSS is minimalist, applying Scrum principles at scale with as few additional roles and ceremonies as possible; Scrum@Scale is modular, extending Scrum through a Scrum-of-Scrums network and an Executive Action Team for impediment removal.

For GenAI programs, all three frameworks require adaptation in three areas: how features are defined for AI systems (model features are not equivalent to software features); how quality is defined and tested (evaluation against a test set replaces unit tests as the primary quality gate); and how cross-team dependencies involving data, compute, and model infrastructure are managed.
Why It Matters

GenAI programs that scale beyond a single team frequently encounter coordination failures: the data team is blocked waiting for the ML team's schema, the infrastructure team has provisioned compute that the model team's training job cannot use because of a library version conflict, and the product team is building features against a model behaviour that has changed in the latest training run. These failures are not unique to AI — they are coordination failures that happen at scale in any complex technical program — but the non-determinism and long cycle times of AI make them more expensive to discover late.

Applying an agile-at-scale framework with appropriate GenAI adaptations addresses these coordination failures structurally, rather than through heroic project management. The framework makes dependencies visible, creates forums for resolving impediments, and establishes a rhythm of delivery that applies to AI deliverables as much as to software features.
Benefits
Structural coordination: framework ceremonies (ART sync, Scrum-of-Scrums) create defined moments for cross-team dependency management.
Consistent rhythm: PI Planning (SAFe), Sprint Planning (LeSS), and Scrum-of-Scrums provide predictable heartbeats that stakeholders and teams can plan around.
Role clarity: defined roles (Product Owner, Scrum Master, System Architect) translate to ML programs, reducing ambiguity about who owns the model quality backlog.
Impediment escalation: all three frameworks have explicit mechanisms for escalating blockers that a single team cannot resolve.
Portfolio integration: all three frameworks have portfolio-level constructs that connect initiative prioritisation to team capacity.
Retrospective culture: built-in retrospectives at team and program level are particularly valuable for GenAI programs where approaches change frequently.
Trade-offs
SAFe overhead: SAFe's full implementation is ceremony-heavy and can consume significant time — PI Planning alone is a 2-day event every 8-12 weeks.
LeSS minimalism: LeSS's single-backlog model requires a highly competent Product Owner who can manage the complexity of ML, data, and product features in one backlog.
Scrum@Scale immaturity: Scrum@Scale is less battle-tested than SAFe or LeSS for large enterprises; fewer consultants and less tooling support.
GenAI mismatch: none of the frameworks natively supports model versioning, evaluation pipelines, or data dependency graphs — these must be grafted on.

Velocity as a metric: story point velocity is a poor measure of GenAI team progress — model improvement (quantitative metric change) is a better sprint goal, but most frameworks have no built-in construct for this.
How It Works
SAFe for GenAI

In SAFe, the Agile Release Train (ART) is the primary delivery vehicle. For a GenAI program, the ART typically consists of: a product/feature team building the end-user interface or integration layer; a model team responsible for training, evaluation, and serving the AI model; a data team managing data pipelines, feature stores, and training data quality; and a platform/infrastructure team managing GPU compute, MLflow/W&B tracking, and deployment infrastructure. The ART is coordinated by a Release Train Engineer (RTE), whose role is analogous to a senior Scrum Master at the program level.

SAFe's Lean Portfolio Management (LPM) layer handles initiative intake and prioritisation for the GenAI portfolio — this maps directly to the portfolio intake processes described in earlier topics. PI Objectives in SAFe must be adapted for GenAI: instead of 'deliver feature X', PI Objectives should be 'reduce hallucination rate to below 3% on production traffic' or 'complete training data quality remediation for 80% of the corpus', which are outcomes tied to model quality metrics.
LeSS for GenAI

LeSS uses a single Product Owner and a single product backlog across all teams. For a GenAI program, this works well when the entire program is building one AI product (e.g., an AI-powered document assistant), because the Product Owner can maintain coherent prioritisation across model, data, and product work in a single backlog. The LeSS Sprint Review is a multi-team event where all teams show their sprint results — for GenAI, the sprint review should include model evaluation results on the held-out test set as a first-class demonstration, not just UI features.

LeSS's Definition of Done needs explicit extension for GenAI: a model feature is only Done when it is trained, evaluated against the acceptance criteria (e.g., passes precision recall threshold), reviewed for safety and bias, and integrated into the serving layer. Without this extended DoD, 'done' means 'trained' but not 'validated' — a frequent source of rework in AI programs.
Scrum@Scale for GenAI

Scrum@Scale uses a Scrum-of-Scrums (SoS) to coordinate dependency removal across teams. For a GenAI program, the SoS agenda should include a standing item for data and model dependencies — which team is blocked on which data asset, which model version, or which infrastructure component. The Executive Action Team (EAT) is the equivalent of a program leadership team and is responsible for removing organisational impediments: securing GPU budget, resolving cross-team HR conflicts, and making architectural decisions that individual teams cannot make. For GenAI, the EAT is also the natural owner of AI ethics and risk decisions that require authority above the individual team level.
GenAI-Specific Adaptations Common to All Frameworks

Regardless of framework, GenAI programs need four structural adaptations. First, a model evaluation cadence: set aside one sprint per PI (SAFe) or a dedicated evaluation week per 4-6 sprints (LeSS/Scrum@Scale) for non-feature evaluation work — comparing model versions, running red-teaming, and assessing safety. Second, nondeterminism in the Definition of Done: 'Done' for a model feature must include a pass/fail evaluation gate with a quantitative threshold, not just code completion. Third, spike capacity: reserve 15-20% of team capacity every sprint for research spikes — experiments whose outcomes are unknown in advance; most frameworks allow spikes but do not explicitly budget for them. Fourth, model versioning as a delivery unit: a 'model version' is the atomic deliverable equivalent of a software release — it has a version number, a model card, evaluation results, and a go/no-go decision. All framework backlogs should track model versions as discrete deliverables.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Comparison of SAFe, LeSS, and Scrum@Scale frameworks applied to GenAI programs, showing structure, GenAI fit, adaptations, and when to use each

content.text: When to Use It
SAFe: large enterprise GenAI programs (50+ people, multiple ARTs, compliance-heavy regulated sectors, existing SAFe adoption in the organisation).
LeSS: 2-8 team GenAI programs building a single AI product with a sophisticated Product Owner; organisations that value simplicity over process.
Scrum@Scale: organisations where Scrum is already the team-level practice and a lightweight scaling layer is preferred over a full framework adoption.
Hybrid: many GenAI programs operate with SAFe or LeSS at the team level combined with portfolio governance processes from this topic series at the program level.
How to Implement
Assess your context: team size, regulatory environment, existing agile maturity, and number of teams. This determines which framework is most appropriate.
Map your GenAI team types (model, data, product, infra) to the framework's team model — identify which teams will be in the same ART/LeSS group/SoS.
Extend the Definition of Done to include model evaluation gates before adopting the framework — retrofitting this later is harder.
Create a model versioning convention before the first PI or sprint — teams need to know how to refer to model versions in backlog items.
Identify the PI Planning / Sprint Planning adaptation needed for data dependencies: data team deliverables must be first-class PI Objectives or Sprint Goals.
Reserve spike capacity (15-20%) in the first PI/sprint cycle before pressure to commit to feature delivery eliminates it.
Train Scrum Masters and RTEs on GenAI-specific impediment types: compute contention, data schema changes, model evaluation failures, and regulatory blockers.
Examples
SAFe PI Objective example for GenAI

# PI 4 Objectives — AI Platform ART
# Q3 2026 (10 sprints, 10 weeks)

## Business Objectives
1. [Committed] Reduce hallucination rate on customer support queries to <3%
   - Metric: measured on held-out eval set (1,000 queries), automated weekly
   - Owner: ML Lead | Confidence: 8/10

2. [Committed] Complete training data quality remediation for top 3 query categories
   - Metric: DQ score ≥0.90 for 'billing', 'account', 'delivery' categories
   - Owner: Data Lead | Confidence: 9/10

3. [Committed] Deploy model serving to 5 additional regional endpoints (EU-West, AP-SE)
   - Metric: p99 latency <2s from each endpoint under 100 rps load
   - Owner: Infra Lead | Confidence: 7/10

4. [Uncommitted] Complete red-team evaluation on adversarial input set
   - Metric: <1% of adversarial inputs cause policy violation
   - Owner: ML Lead | Confidence: 6/10

## Architecture Epic (Enabler)
- Deploy model evaluation pipeline integrated with CI/CD
  (blocker for Committed Obj 1 automated measurement)

## Risks
- Training data for 'account' category blocked on Legal DPA review (ETA: Week 2)
- EU regulatory compliance checklist not yet received (ETA: Week 3)

## Dependencies
- Depends on Platform Team: GPU cluster expansion to 32xA100 by Sprint 2
- Depends on Security Team: pen test sign-off before EU endpoint go-live
LeSS Definition of Done for GenAI features

# Definition of Done — AI Document Assistant (LeSS)
# Updated: 2026-07-19 | All teams apply this DoD

## Software Component Done
- [ ] Code reviewed and merged to main branch
- [ ] Unit tests: ≥90% coverage, all passing
- [ ] Integration tests: end-to-end scenarios passing
- [ ] Deployed to staging environment
- [ ] Performance tested: p99 latency ≤2s at 50 rps

## Model Component Done (additional for model features)
- [ ] Model trained on approved dataset version {dataset_version}
- [ ] Evaluation passed: precision ≥0.88, recall ≥0.85 on held-out test set
- [ ] Hallucination rate ≤3% on evaluation set (automated check)
- [ ] Red-team check: no policy violations on adversarial test set
- [ ] Bias evaluation: no demographic disparity >5pp on fairness test set
- [ ] Model card updated with: version, training data hash, eval results, known limitations
- [ ] Model serving load tested: ≥100 rps at ≤2s p99
- [ ] Go/No-Go decision recorded in model registry with approver name

## Data Component Done (for data pipeline features)
- [ ] Data quality score ≥0.90 on target dataset
- [ ] Lineage documented in data catalog
- [ ] PII scan complete: no unlabelled PII in training set
- [ ] Data drift test passed vs baseline distribution

## Security/Compliance Done (for all changes)
- [ ] Secrets scan passed (no credentials in code)
- [ ] Prompt injection test run (for LLM-facing components)
- [ ] Change documented in AI governance log
Real-World Example

A European financial services firm adopting SAFe for their GenAI portfolio ran into friction during the first PI Planning event: the ML team's objectives were expressed as research goals ('explore RLHF for alignment improvement') rather than committed business outcomes. The Release Train Engineer worked with the ML lead before PI 2 to reframe ML objectives in terms of measurable model quality improvements: 'reduce factual error rate from 4.2% to <3% on the product liability query set'. This change required the evaluation infrastructure to exist before the PI — a dependency that became a Program Increment Enabler feature tracked on the ART backlog. By PI 3, the ML team was operating comfortably within SAFe's commitment model and the broader organisation had confidence in what the AI teams were delivering each increment.
Common Mistakes
Applying a chosen framework without adapting the Definition of Done for AI deliverables — model features are not done when the code is merged; they are done when the evaluation passes.
Treating model training runs as sprint commitments — training completion time is highly variable and should be planned as a spike, not a committed story.
Neglecting spike capacity in PI Planning — without dedicated allocation, research experiments get squeezed out by feature delivery pressure.
Using velocity (story points) as the primary team health metric for GenAI teams — model improvement metrics are a better reflection of progress.
Assigning data dependencies as 'assumptions' in PI Objectives rather than tracking them as explicit inter-team dependencies in the ART board.
Having the ML team operate on a different sprint cadence than the rest of the ART — synchronisation requires a shared sprint boundary even if the work nature differs.
Best Practices
Extend the Definition of Done for model and data components before the first sprint — this is the most important GenAI adaptation.
Treat model versions as the delivery unit: version number, model card, evaluation results, and go/no-go decision are the unit of acceptance.
Reserve 15-20% of ART/team capacity per PI for spikes — research without this reservation will be crowded out within 2-3 PIs.
Create a shared model registry visible to all teams in the ART — this is the authoritative source for which model version is in which environment.
Educate Scrum Masters and RTEs on GenAI impediment types: compute queue contention, data schema breaks, evaluation regressions are common blockers not in standard Scrum training.
Hold a dedicated 'model evaluation review' as part of Sprint Review — this gives the Product Owner and stakeholders visibility into AI quality that a feature demonstration alone cannot provide.
Performance & Scalability

At large scale (5+ ARTs with GenAI components), PI Planning becomes a significant coordination challenge if every ART includes a model team with its own evaluation pipeline and model registry. Consider a shared AI Platform ART that provides model serving, evaluation infrastructure, and ML tooling as a service to feature ARTs — reducing the coordination surface area and enabling feature ARTs to focus on product delivery without managing model infrastructure. This mirrors the platform team pattern in software engineering applied to ML infrastructure.
Tooling & Ecosystem
SAFe: Jira Align / Rally for ART backlog, PI Planning, and dependency tracking.
LeSS: standard Jira or Linear with multi-team sprint planning; no specialist tooling required.
Scrum@Scale: Jira with multi-team board views; Scrum@Scale can be implemented with standard Scrum tooling.
MLflow / Weights & Biases: model registry and experiment tracking — the equivalent of a version control system for models.
DVC (Data Version Control): data pipeline versioning that integrates with sprint-level delivery tracking.
GitHub Actions / GitLab CI: automated evaluation pipeline triggered by model commits — the 'CI/CD for ML' that makes DoD automated.

Security & Reliability
PI Planning produces public PI Objectives that may reveal sensitive model capabilities or dataset sources — classify PI artefacts appropriately.
Model registry access should be role-controlled: read access for all ART teams, write access only for the ML team — prevents accidental model version overwrite.
In SAFe, Inspect and Adapt sessions may surface model quality problems that require security review before broader discussion — define the confidentiality protocol for AI quality reviews.
Scrum@Scale's EAT should have an explicit charter item for AI risk and ethics decisions — these decisions need authority above the team level and a clear accountability owner.
Key Takeaways
SAFe, LeSS, and Scrum@Scale all apply to GenAI programs but require explicit adaptation for model features, evaluation quality gates, and data dependencies.
The most critical GenAI adaptation for any framework is extending the Definition of Done to include model evaluation gates with quantitative thresholds.
Model versions — not features — are the atomic delivery unit for AI teams: a version has a number, a model card, evaluation results, and a go/no-go decision.
Reserve 15-20% of team capacity per sprint/PI for research spikes — without explicit reservation, feature pressure crowds out experimentation.
SAFe is most appropriate for large enterprise GenAI programs; LeSS for single-product medium teams; Scrum@Scale for Scrum-native organisations wanting a lightweight coordination layer.
A shared AI Platform ART (SAFe) or platform team (LeSS) reduces coordination overhead for model serving, evaluation infrastructure, and ML tooling across feature teams.
Story point velocity is a poor health metric for GenAI teams — model quality improvement metrics (evaluation score trend) are a better signal.
